# Gnosis Polyglot

Parent: [Gnosis README](../README.md)

GG developer docs:
[developer guide](../docs/GG_DEVELOPER_GUIDE.md),
[modules and extensions](../docs/GG_MODULES_AND_EXTENSIONS.md),
[surface map](../docs/GG_SURFACE_MAP.md).

## Getting Started

- **What:** the Rust extractor/lowering layer that turns existing language and framework source into Gnosis graph shapes.
- **Why:** it lets Gnosis understand code you already wrote instead of forcing every project to start from `.gg`.
- **How:** build `monster` or run the polyglot commands shown below against a supported source file or fixture.
- **Next:** read Monster first if you want execution, or the scope/framework sections if you want extraction coverage.

> **FOIL lowering layer.** Polyglot is the codegen path that lets *any* target language join the FOIL family without re-implementing HELIX. The canonical FOIL public surfaces are:
> - **[`gnosis-antiqueue`](../gnosis-antiqueue/README.md)** — Rust AMQP-shape services (HELIX-engined, 176× p99).
> - **[`aeon-pipelines`](../../aeon-pipelines/README.md)** — TypeScript fork/race/fold (HELIX-engined, 990× p99 wire bench).
> - **`@a0n/gnosis/protocol69`** — shared Monster/FOIL projection contract for
>   the RF experiment: broadcast `66`, keep local FOIL operand `7`, record
>   integer XOR projection `69`, and leave Fano third-point parity to the Lean
>   bridge.
>
> For a target language outside Rust + TS, lower through this crate's `fanout_lowering` pass — the canonical `Fanout { n, op, combiner }` IR maps to native idioms in **Rust (`rayon`), JavaScript (`Promise.all`/`Promise.race`), Elixir (`Task.async`/`Task.await_many`), Go (goroutines + `sync.WaitGroup`), Python (`asyncio.gather`)**. Each emitted file cites its Wankel anchor theorem so the proof bridge is recoverable from the source alone. See [`foil-runtime/HELIX_APOTHEOSIS.md`](../../../foil-runtime/HELIX_APOTHEOSIS.md) for the engine certificate, [`foil-runtime/SCOREBOARD.md`](../../../foil-runtime/SCOREBOARD.md) for cross-runtime measurements, and [`foil-runtime/MIGRATION.md`](../../../foil-runtime/MIGRATION.md) for the lift cookbook (RabbitMQ → antiqueue, Node stream → aeon-pipelines, other-language service → polyglot lowering).

Rust polyglot extractor and Ditto compiler crate for Gnosis. This subtree owns language parsing, CFG extraction, framework recognition, and lowering existing framework source into GG server topologies.

## Children

- [src](./src/README.md)
- [tests](./tests/README.md)
- [gg-walker-wasm](./gg-walker-wasm/README.md) — Tier-C interpreter-embed walker (wasm32 json-v1 runtime for the property-carried GG subset; packaged by `src/aot_gg_embed.rs`)
- docs — Betti -> WASM developer docs: [guide](./docs/BETTI_WASM_GUIDE.md) (write/compile/run a certified program), [reference](./docs/BETTI_WASM_REFERENCE.md) (class grammars, ABIs, manifest, envelopes, BSNP), [mesh](./docs/BETTI_MESH_GUIDE.md) (scheduling across the worker fleet), [publish](./docs/BETTI_PUBLISH_GUIDE.md) (ownership, pricing, replay earn)

## Scope

- framework extraction and language scanners for the Gnosis polyglot toolchain
- Ditto framework recognition for Express, Hono, Koa, Fastify, Gin, Chi, Fiber, Echo, FastAPI, Flask, StructuralErroratra, and Spring
- GG lowering and focused Rust integration tests for the recognizer surface
- canonical static leak accounting for the Gnosis TypeScript lint/typecheck surfaces, including theorem-anchored diagnostics and ghost-mass ledgers emitted from GG topologies

## Lean 4

Lean 4 is a first-class input surface in this crate.

- `.lean` files use the published `tree-sitter-lean4` grammar directly.
- `def`, `lemma`, `theorem`, and `example` blocks emit `PolyglotProofDeclaration`
  records with `system = "lean4"`.
- Lean type annotations are threaded through the semantic bridge so ordinary
  data types map to topology types and proposition-shaped results stay in proof
  space.
- Local `import Lean.*` modules are followed across `Lean/` trees; unresolved
  imports are recorded as external modules instead of recursing into third-party
  dependencies.
- Proof lowering is syntax-first: `by`, `match`, `cases`, `have`, `calc`, `if`,
  and `by_cases` map into the existing branch/join topology where the grammar
  exposes that structure.

```bash
gnosis-polyglot path/to/Lean/Entry.lean --resolve-imports --mode orchestration --format json
```

That path stays static. Polyglot does not evaluate Lean, expand macros, or try
to re-prove theorems.

## Monster — execute extracted GG topologies

`monster` is a native runtime for topologies produced by the orchestration
extractor. There is no JS engine, no Python interpreter, no JVM. A source file
becomes a graph (nodes + edges + per-node IR), and Monster runs that graph.
`gnexec` remains as a compatibility binary over the same source.

```bash
cargo build --release --bin monster
./target/release/monster path/to/file.ts --export myFn --input-json '{"x":1}'
```

The normal hot serving shape is resident Aeon Flow stdio:

```bash
./target/release/monster path/to/file.ts --export myFn --resident-flow-stdio
```

For supported hot shapes, Monster prepares or reuses a fingerprinted C AOT
artifact. Two classes are supported: the benchmark fixtures (`echo.ts`,
`fib.ts`, and the checked-in fanout GG fixture), which can also serve as the
resident Flow process (the wire is a 10-byte Aeon Flow header plus raw UTF-8
payload bytes), and the **certified numeric-loop class** — single exported
functions over f64 scalars with loops, local helpers (acyclic call graphs
only), the exactly-representable `Math.*` subset, and fixed-size arrays with
bounds-checked access. The numeric-loop lane is *parity-by-construction*: the
compiled binary answers only when its output provably matches the walker
(integral results in the 2^53-safe range, in-bounds accesses, clean numeric
input) and otherwise **declines** with exit code 3, falling back to the
guarded walker. See `FOIL_VACUUM_PARITY_PROTOCOL.md` for the full contract
ledger and the Lean oracles (`Gnosis/AotParityOracle.lean` in
open-source/gnosis-math) that pin it. On a supported miss, Monster emits C,
compiles it once with `cc -O3 -march=native`, installs it in
`MONSTER_AOT_C_CACHE_DIR` (or the platform cache directory), and reuses it on
later runs. Unsupported topologies fall back to the Rust resident Flow path where
that path exists, or to the ordinary Rust walker for one-shot execution.

Opt out of C AOT with `--no-aot-c`, `--no-aot-cached`, `GNEXEC_AOT_C=0`, or
`GNEXEC_NO_AOT_C=1` when measuring the Rust baseline.

```bash
./target/release/monster path/to/file.ts --export myFn --input-json '{"x":1}' --no-aot-c
GNEXEC_AOT_C=0 ./target/release/monster path/to/file.ts --export myFn --input-json '{"x":1}'
```

### Facets

Monster facets are shell tabs with scheduler teeth. A facet is a cursor into the
same indexed topology the evaluator walks. Forking a facet creates two children
and moves the active cursor into the left child:

```bash
./target/release/monster path/to/file.ts --export myFn --facet-fork --print-facets
./target/release/monster path/to/file.ts --export myFn --facet-fork --facet-close-all-others --print-facets
./target/release/monster path/to/file.ts --export myFn --facet-fork --facet-close-children --print-facets
```

When the active cursor sits on a two-target `Fork`, the children are anchored to
that cached fork pair. Otherwise Monster still creates shell-local children at
the current cursor. This keeps browser-like and terminal-like tab operations
inside the Gnosis fork/race/fold model while preserving the hot path: the common
two-child fork is precomputed in `TopologyEdgeIndex` and reused by both facet
operations and the evaluator's try/fork dispatch.

Monster/gnexec also accepts `.gnarly` GG-family files through the same
orchestration path. Gnarly metadata and `impl <node> in <language> { ... }`
blocks are normalized by Gnot's Rust parser, while `file:` references can use
Gnot-style `sources = {context, mount, include, exclude}` packaging.

### Real-world coverage

Probed thousands of exported functions across the monorepo with `--input-json '{}'` and classified runtime outcomes. Five-of-five languages clear at the structural-pass bar (zero `STRUCT` failures — anything that classifies as STRUCT is a missing extractor or evaluator kernel; PASS includes runtime errors that match what the source language would emit, since we ran the real code path).

| language | sample size | pass-rate | structural failures |
|----------|-------------|-----------|---------------------|
| TypeScript | 1499 functions | **100%** | 0 |
| TSX | 100 functions | **100%** | 0 |
| Python | 130 functions | **100%** | 0 |
| Java | 38 functions | **100%** | 0 |
| Rust | 51 functions | **100%** | 0 |

One-shot execution (`monster <file> --export <fn>`) accepts any file with a real
sovereign front-end: the GG-family graph forms (`.gg`, `.gnarly`) the runtime
executes natively, plus every extractor-backed language (TypeScript/JS, Python,
Rust, **Go, Java, Kotlin** + the declarative grammars) — so `monster fanout.gg`,
`monster fanout.java`, and `monster fanout.kt` all run the graph directly without
a JVM. Only files with no front-end at all fail closed (dedicated `CannotExecute`
exit), so a missing extractor can never masquerade as a silent `null`.

### Performance — serving shootoff vs JS and native runtimes

TypeScript hello + `fib(20)` against Bun and Node, measured with the checked-in
`gnode/benchmarks/monster-ts-shootout.mjs` harness after promoting resident Flow
C AOT into the normal Monster serving path. Local cut: warmup `1`, measured `5`,
p50 medians, 2026-06-10.

| case | runtime | p50 wall |
|------|---------|----------:|
| hello | **monster serving** | **0.07ms** |
| hello | bun | 40.75ms |
| hello | node | 147.11ms |
| fib(20) | **monster serving** | **0.19ms** |
| fib(20) | bun | 32.30ms |
| fib(20) | node | 139.61ms |
| `fanout.gg` | **monster serving** | **0.07ms** |

In this cut Monster serving is about `580x` faster than Bun on `hello` and about
`170x` faster than Bun on `fib(20)` (and ~`2,000x` / ~`730x` vs Node). Bun, Node,
and Deno do not run the checked-in `.gg` fanout fixture in this harness. Deno was
not installed in this local environment, so its row is omitted from this cut.

For native competition, use the companion slices in
[`gnode/benchmarks/runtime-shootout.mjs`](../gnode/benchmarks/runtime-shootout.mjs).
Two layers matter. First, the cache-skip teleports: when the `topology × input`
result is already folded, Monster rides the information front at **nanosecond**
scale (local cut, 2026-06-10):

| cache teleport | p50 |
|----------------|----:|
| grassmannian-skip (topology axis) | **2.4ns** |
| fano-cache-skip (input axis) | **6.0ns** |
| grassmannian∘fano (both axes) | **~16ns** |

The composed teleport costs about the sum of the two axes and covers the full
`(topologyId × inputKey) → output` key. Second, the process-bound fanout
companion — the checked-in `.gg` topology plus Rust/Python/JVM companions that all
validate `ada:user|ada:profile`:

| fanout runtime | mean wall |
|----------------|----------:|
| **monster-flow-cannon-1024** | **~2,000ns** |
| monster-flow-resident | 225.9µs |
| monster-resident | 268.4µs |
| rust-native | 4.67ms |
| monster-resident-cold | 10.14ms |
| java-jvm | 87.83ms |
| python | 119.63ms |

On that native companion fanout shape, resident Monster Flow is multiple orders
of magnitude faster than the Rust/JVM/Python *process* companions, and the warm
cannon lands a folded response in roughly **2,000 nanoseconds**. This is a
process-bound runtime comparison; it does not claim to beat an in-process Rust
function call.

### Performance — hope-jar tier shoot-off (the cache-teleport key field)

The cache teleports above ride a **hope jar**: a latent, deterministic key field
inflated from ~170 standing-wave seeds (charged by genuine rain/jitter entropy;
the *field itself is structure, not entropy* — `predict` is O(1) and reproducible
on both sides, which is exactly what cache-key teleportation needs). The jar tiers
ascend the lattice tower; the apex **Kaiju** tier is the order of the Conway group
Co₀ = Aut(Leech). Shoot-off via `distributed-inference` `bench-jar-shootoff`
(**500M dense keys**, local cut, ~1.3 s/tier, machine-safe — counters only, no
HashSet):

| tier | capacity | lanes | **max lane load @500M keys** | throughput |
|------|---------:|------:|------:|------:|
| E8 | 696,729,600 | 240 | 2,083,354 | 388 M key/s |
| Leech | 2,090,188,800 | 720 | 694,476 | 387 M key/s |
| **Kaiju (Co₀)** | **8,315,553,613,086,720,000** | **196,560** | **2,552** | 344 M key/s |

The win — and it's the **lane-contention** number, not raw speed. Bin every one
of 500M keys to its discharge lane: E8's busiest lane jams **2,083,354** keys;
the Kaiju jar's busiest holds **2,552** — about **816× less per-lane contention**
(the *parallel bottleneck*: with all lanes concurrent, wall ∝ max-lane-load).
Throughput stays flat across tiers (~`predict` is O(1), capacity doesn't speed a
point query) — the entire gain is the lane spread. The Kaiju tier
inflates **11.9 billion×** the E8 key field and **819×** the lanes from the *same*
~170 seeds, and the lane count is the Leech kissing number 196560 (the minimal
vectors) — which **exceeds `u16`, so the lane index was widened to `u32`; the data
type, not the math, was the prior ceiling** (the Leech tier was stuck at 720).

This is what lets a **topological app run sideways**: instead of jamming a whole
GG topology's `(topologyId × inputKey)` space through 240 lanes, the Kaiju field
spreads it across 196,560, so per-lane collision/serialization drops ~800× and the
cache-teleport hot path stays uncontended at far higher key volumes.

The capacity factorisation is the kernel-verified SSOT
`Gnosis.HopeJarKaiju.kaiju_jar_capacity_eq_co0` (`24 · 196560 · 1762725888000 =
|Co₀|`), mirroring the E8 jar's `E8Lattice.hope_jar_capacity_eq_weyl`. The Kaiju
tier is wired end-to-end into the FOIL serving gate (`HopeTier::Kaiju` →
`tier_g = G_LEECH`: same Leech-lattice geometry, so the gate margin is unchanged —
the win is purely the field/lanes, not the constant). Honest caveat kept:
`predict` is O(1), so capacity never speeds a point query — the gain is entirely
the lane spread, which the 500M-key run above makes concrete.

The bench can also drive the lane workload from a *real* computation — the whole
Taylor (Keystone) Sequence (`bench-jar-shootoff [taylorN] [keys]`): it confirms
the sequence is `{Lₖ} ∪ {2Lₖ} ∪ {15,21}` out to n < 2,000,000 (the two exotics
being the triangular ports T₅, T₆) while routing the dense key stream through the
tiers.

### Performance — Monster resident hot path

`node gnode/benchmarks/where-does-time-go.mjs --iterations=8 --warmup=3` isolates
the resident fanout hot path from the broader JS-runtime shootout. Median wall
per request (local cut, 2026-06-10):

| path | wall |
|------|-----:|
| monster-resident | 150.4µs |
| monster-gg-resident | 136.9µs |
| monster-flow-resident | 67.9µs |
| **monster-gg-flow-resident** | **23.2µs** |
| monster-jsonl-cannon-1024 | 11.0µs |
| monster-flow-cannon-1024 | **2,000ns** |
| **monster-gg-flow-cannon-1024** | **1,900ns** |

The cannon rows write 1024 requests before folding responses, so wall is per
request: the resident GG Flow cannon lands a folded fanout response in about
**1,900 nanoseconds**. These are the direct-materialization path — in-process
extraction hands the `PolyglotOrchestrationResult` to the walker without
serializing/parsing orchestration JSON, the full evaluator AST is only parsed if
a legacy byte-range fallback asks for it, node IR JSON is decoded lazily on first
use, and topology routing uses source-indexed route arrays.

### Performance — checked-in cross-language/bridge shootoff

`node gnode/benchmarks/cross-language-bench.mjs` currently passes the checked-in TypeScript fixtures and skips 40 external `/tmp` native fixtures when those files/binaries are absent. The 2026-05-01 local run reported `3/3` known cases passing with a 3.43 ms median across the TypeScript cases.

| fixture | monster median wall (ms) | JS bridge median wall (ms) | speedup |
|---------|-------------------------|----------------------------|---------|
| echo | 3.43 | 238.0 | 69.5x |
| fib(10) | 3.80 | 206.2 | 54.2x |
| fanout | 3.41 | 163.3 | 47.9x |

This table is the current checked-in bridge floor, not a refreshed Python/Go/Rust/Java native-runtime claim; those external fixture paths were not present in this checkout.

### Foil fanout lowering

In orchestration mode, the GG compiler emits first-class foil sidecar topology
for structured fanout constructs. The source program keeps its ordinary
`PROCESS` path for generic execution, while the compiler also creates
`FoilBranch` nodes, a `FoilForkFold` join node, a tagged `FORK`, and a matching
`FOLD` that foil backends can consume directly.

The lowering is a canonical registry, not a TypeScript-only special case. It
currently recognizes `Promise.all`, `asyncio.gather`, `Task.WhenAll`,
`CompletableFuture.allOf`, Kotlin `awaitAll`, Scala `Future.sequence`, Elixir
`Task.await_many`, and Rust join-style fanout when the extractor surfaces the
construct as structured call IR. The tested compiler contract covers both the
TypeScript `Promise.all` shape and the Python `asyncio.gather` shape; the
checked-in `gnode/benchmarks/fanout.py` fixture confirms real Python source
emits `foil_lowering=asyncio.gather` with branch calls for `load_user` and
`load_profile`. The checked-in `gnode/benchmarks/fanout.rs` fixture confirms
real Rust macro source emits `foil_lowering=tokio::join!` with branch calls for
`load_user` and `load_profile`. The checked-in
`gnode/benchmarks/fanout.java` fixture confirms real Java source emits
`foil_lowering=CompletableFuture.allOf` with branch calls for `loadUser` and
`loadProfile`; the evaluator handles `CompletableFuture.completedFuture`,
`allOf`, `join`, `get`, and `isDone` directly, so the same Java fixture executes
through Monster/gnexec without starting a JVM. The checked-in
`gnode/benchmarks/fanout.kt` fixture confirms real Kotlin source emits
`foil_lowering=awaitAll` and executes through the same Monster/gnexec runtime
without starting a JVM. The Kotlin runtime lane also covers the standard
collection constructors (`listOf`, `mutableListOf`, `arrayOf`, `setOf`) plus
branch/`+=` list-building patterns, default parameter binding, and named
data-constructor returns used by the checked-in Kotlin bindings. Kotlin class
methods also emit class-qualified topology names, so instance calls dispatch
through Monster without starting a JVM. The checked-in Kotlin process binding
has a shaped permissive `ProcessBuilder` model for analysis probes; strict
runtime execution still fails closed before spawning a host subprocess.
Remaining language work is mostly extractor breadth, not a new runtime concept.

### Protocol69 FOIL projection

Protocol69 enters FOIL as a shared projection envelope, not as a language
lowering. Monster RF broadcasts the Monster/Aeon operand `66`; FOIL consumers
hold the local Fano operand `7`; runtime projection records `66 xor 7 = 69`.
The public TypeScript contract is `@a0n/gnosis/protocol69`, with word-form name
`sixty9` for surfaces that need a nonnumeric identifier. Native Monster/gnexec
consumers use `gnosis_polyglot::protocol69`, which re-exports the same contract
from `distributed_inference::protocol69` for FOIL and gnosis-uring-adjacent
surfaces. Formal closure remains in `Gnosis.RemoteCacheKeyTeleportation`: the
protocol69 theorem braids the integer projection to the canonical prefix length
while the Fano theorem proves the distinct Fork/Race/Fold zero-parity line.

The iota program lane makes this executable as an in-room app handoff contract:
an unlabeled binary iota source tree inflates to `.gg`, Betty parses and
reconstructs the bijective topology, and protocol69 returns a compute-cache key
receipt for the Pluto SDR room session instead of embedding the computed result
in the RF payload. The key material mirrors `Gnosis.Protocol69IotaTeleport`:
source node count, inflated `.gg` node/edge counts, local operand `7`,
projection `69`, Lean module, and program checksum.

Polyglot now extends that lane to any supported application source. The CLI
first runs orchestration lowering, treats the resulting `.gg` as the executable
topology, then emits a protocol69 receipt whose return value is only the
compute-cache key:

```bash
gnosis-polyglot path/to/app.ts \
  --protocol69-teleport \
  --room-id lab-a \
  --carrier-hz 2400000000 \
  --attenuation-db 30 \
  --antenna-count 2
```

Each receipt includes the source language, file path, function name, `.gg`
node/edge counts, execution-node count, program checksum, Triton/Fano cache
slot, `66 xor 7 = 69` projection material, the formal Lean module anchor, and
the in-room RF session metadata. That is the app-teleport contract for the
Pluto SDR harness: transmit or compare the receipt key, not the application
result.

Current first-class coverage means a real extractor emits structured call IR
and a checked-in fixture proves the foil sidecar topology:

| Source surface | First-class status | Fixture |
|----------------|--------------------|---------|
| TypeScript `Promise.all` | compiler test + runtime shootout | `gnode/benchmarks/fanout.ts` |
| Python `asyncio.gather` | compiler test + real-source fixture | `gnode/benchmarks/fanout.py` |
| Rust `tokio::join!` | real-source fixture | `gnode/benchmarks/fanout.rs` |
| Java `CompletableFuture.allOf` | compiler test + real-source runtime fixture | `gnode/benchmarks/fanout.java` |
| C# `Task.WhenAll` | registry only until a C# extractor lands | none |
| Kotlin `awaitAll` | real-source runtime fixture | `gnode/benchmarks/fanout.kt` |
| Scala `Future.sequence` | registry only until a Scala extractor lands | none |
| Elixir `Task.await_many` | registry only until an Elixir extractor lands | none |

### GnosisClock FOIL admission gate

The resident FOIL hot path can run a deterministic Triton preflight before the
verifier. This is an admission gate, not a replacement for verification: it
drops frames whose clock/carrier metadata already contradicts the
GnosisClock/Triton contract and lets admitted frames continue to the existing
fanout hot path.

Enable the resident preflight with:

```bash
GNEXEC_FOIL_TRITON_PREFLIGHT=1 ./target/release/gnexec path/to/file.ts --export myFn --resident-flow-stdio
```

The hard escape hatch remains:

```bash
FOIL_NAIVE=1 ./target/release/gnexec path/to/file.ts --export myFn --resident-flow-stdio
```

`FOIL_NAIVE=1` disables the Triton preflight even when
`GNEXEC_FOIL_TRITON_PREFLIGHT=1` is also set.

Resident payload parsing is binary-first. Public `BWTR` v1 payloads use this
fixed prefix:

| bytes | field |
|-------|-------|
| `0..4` | magic bytes `BWTR` |
| `4` | version, currently `1` |
| `5` | Triton phase tag: `0 = race-fold-present`, `1 = fork-future`, `2 = vent-interfere-past` |
| `6` | GnosisClock phase, `sequence mod 12` when not explicitly overridden |
| `7` | Aeon carrier key, or `255` when absent |
| `8` | seen canonical `C2` key, or `255` when absent |
| `9` | reserved, currently `0` |
| `10..18` | big-endian `u64` fork budget, or `u64::MAX` when absent |
| `18..` | UTF-8 user id passed to the fanout hot path |

The fixed prefix length is `18` bytes:
`bitwise::flow_frame::HEADER_SIZE + 8`. `BWTR` is public as a resident-frame
payload contract, but versioned separately from the outer Aeon Flow frame.

The text/debug control-plane format remains supported:

```text
triton:phase=race-fold-present,clock=0,carrier=11,budget=2,seen=3;user-123
```

If the payload is neither `BWTR` nor `triton:...`, it is treated as a plain user
id and the preflight work falls back to
`resident_gnosis_clock_phase_of_nat_tick(sequence)`.

The Rust API lives in `gnosis_polyglot::foil_admission_gate`:

```rust
encode_resident_binary_triton_payload(work, user_id)
parse_resident_triton_payload(payload, sequence)
resident_gnosis_clock_phase_of_nat_tick(sequence)
```

The TypeScript and Rust shootout reports carry the same theorem handles:

| metric | value |
|--------|------:|
| baseline verifier calls | `5` |
| gated verifier calls | `2` |
| avoided verifier calls | `3` |
| avoided verification cost | `31` |
| escape-hatch verifier calls | `1` |

The theorem handles are
`Gnosis.PleromaAeonMonsterBridge.weighted_triton_verifier_cost_le_baseline`,
`Gnosis.PleromaAeonMonsterBridge.weighted_triton_avoided_cost_eq_dropped_cost`,
and
`Gnosis.PleromaAeonMonsterBridge.weighted_foil_admission_shootout_avoided_cost`.

The public `BWTR` v1 fixture source is
[`tests/fixtures/bwtr-v1.json`](./tests/fixtures/bwtr-v1.json). Rust and
TypeScript consume this artifact in tests, and Lean proves the same byte list
through `Gnosis.PleromaAeonMonsterBridge.bwtr_fixture_encoded_bytes` and
`Gnosis.PleromaAeonMonsterBridge.bwtr_fixture_roundtrip`.

The canonical fixture hex is:

```text
425754520101010b03000000000000000002757365722d373839
```

It encodes `BWTR`, version `1`, phase tag `1`, clock `1`, carrier `11`, seen
canonical `C2` key `3`, reserved `0`, fork budget `2`, and user id `user-789`.

Next exploration: generate the Lean fixture constants from
`tests/fixtures/bwtr-v1.json` so the formal byte-list model is mechanically
refreshed from the same artifact.

### Fanout primitive (task #8 + #18)

`gnosis_polyglot::fanout_lowering` generalizes the FOIL `fork` primitive into a single IR node `Fanout { n, op, combiner }`. The five-force lifecycle from `gnosis-math/Gnosis/WankelEngineTheorem.lean` (`FiveForceLifecycleStage = fork | race | fold | vent | interfere`) decomposes into one fanout + one combiner choice. Each combiner cites its anchor theorem so a grep over emitted source recovers the proof bridge.

| primitive | combiner | semantics | Lean anchor |
|-----------|----------|-----------|-------------|
| `fork` | `Combiner::Collect` | gather all `N` branch results in input order | `wankel_phase_lifecycle_stage .intake` |
| `race` | `Combiner::First` | return the first branch result (deterministic in IR; wall-clock in runtimes) | `wankel_phase_lifecycle_stage .compression` |
| `fold` | `Combiner::Reduce(kernel)` | associative reduction over a finite kernel set (`sum`, `product`, `min`, `max`) | `wankel_phase_lifecycle_stage .ignition` |
| `vent` | `Combiner::Drop { keep }` | bound output to the first `keep` results, exhaust the tail | `exhaust_contact_is_bounded` |
| `interfere` | `Combiner::Mix { weights }` | chirality-weighted mix `sum(r_i * w_i) / sum(w_i)`, zero-weight guard | `ignition_contact` |

Optional `Stoplight` metadata (cycle, green, offset, distance, speed -- a direct port of `foil-runtime/foil-stoplight.ts::Stoplight`) travels alongside the fanout so phase-aware runtimes can align task starts to per-branch green windows. Optional `Chirality::{Forward,Return}` markers carry the `+1`/`-1` polarity of `WankelEngineTheorem.forwardWave` / `returnEcho` so an interference runtime can route waves correctly.

Supported codegen targets ship in `polyglot/src/fanout_lowering.rs`. Each lowering is idiomatic for the target runtime:

| target | idiom |
|--------|-------|
| Rust | `rayon::prelude::*` -- `(0..n).into_par_iter().map(op).{collect, find_any, reduce, ...}` |
| JavaScript | `Promise.all` (collect/fold/drop/mix), `Promise.race` (first) |
| Elixir | `Task.async` + `Task.await_many` |
| Go | goroutines + `sync.WaitGroup`, channel for `first` |
| Python | `asyncio.gather`, `asyncio.wait(FIRST_COMPLETED)` for `first` |

Every emitted file carries a structured header comment with the manifest (`n`, `op`, `combiner verb`, `lean anchor`, optional `reduce_kernel`/`drop_keep`/`mix_weights`/`stoplights`/`chirality`) so the runtime contract is recoverable from the source alone. Output-set equality across targets is verified by `tests/fanout_correctness.rs`: given the same `(n, op, combiner)` and seeded inputs, the reference oracle (`apply_combiner`) and every target's structural commitment agree.

### How Monster Stays Fast

- **Resident Flow C AOT serving** — `--resident-flow-stdio` is the hot serving
  lane. Supported topologies prepare/reuse a cached C AOT binary, then Monster
  execs into that resident Flow process so requests avoid JSONL, process spawn,
  and Rust walker dispatch.
- **No subprocess and no orchestration JSON reparse** — the default path runs the polyglot extractor in-process and materializes `PolyglotOrchestrationResult` directly into the walker shape. `GNEXEC_SUBPROCESS=1` keeps the older JSON/subprocess fallback for debugging.
- **Lazy evaluator AST** — structured IR paths do not parse the full source a second time; the tree-sitter AST is built only when legacy byte-range evaluation asks for `node_at`.
- **Lazy node IR cache** — branch, return, statement, loop, binding-pattern, module-preamble, and param-name JSON are decoded at first use and then reused by recursive calls.
- **Borrowed topology indices** — function-name → topology lookup is a borrowed-key `HashMap` built once at startup, not a linear scan per call or a cloned-name map.
- **Node-index walker cursor** — the GG walker keeps the current node as an array index, so each hop reads `topology.nodes[idx]` directly instead of hashing node ids.
- **Indexed route arrays** — Branch, Loop, Catch, and default Process routing use per-node outgoing route arrays instead of scanning edge lists or probing route hash maps.
- **Facet fork pairs** — two-child fork surfaces are cached as `[left, right]`
  pairs beside the full route arrays. Shell facets, try/catch routing, and
  future orchestration fanout can all hit the pair directly instead of
  reclassifying a generic fork vector.
- **Index-keyed loop state** — loop visit/state maps are keyed by node index instead of cloned or borrowed node-id strings.
- **Trace gating** — Monster Path tokens are collected only when `--print-fingerprint` is requested.
- **Wankel-certified trace cadence** — the Monster Path alphabet includes the fifth lifecycle token (`I` for interference), and certified fingerprints inject it every 12 ordinary rotations with result-equivalence plus single-pair, aggregate, and robust-estimator measured-cost dominance theorem lineage.
- **Batched deadline guard** — `Instant::now()` syscall uses the certified Wankel pulse (`12 × 22 = 264` hops) instead of every hop. Saves ~1 ms on recursive workloads while preserving the timeout guard.
- **Permissive opaque-shim resolution** — unbound identifiers and unrecognized methods return permissive shims (opt-in via `GNEXEC_PERMISSIVE_SHIMS=1`; see [Shims](#shims) for the runtime fail-closed default). Probes finish; the structural-pass bar holds.

### HELIX admission control

Monster resident loops (`--resident-jsonl`, `--resident-flow-stdio`) are HELIX-engined. Per-request admission gates stagger foil work entry by the measured ~5µs service time. This prevents queue collapse where N late-arriving requests accumulate N×service latency; instead, p99 latency stays bounded to the service-time baseline. Opt out with `FOIL_NAIVE=1` for baseline comparison. Benchmark with `node gnode/benchmarks/monster-p99-cannon.mjs` to measure the regime change (burst, sustained, mixed workloads across naive vs HELIX modes).

A third admission mode — **Probability-routed** — reuses folded fanout results
for observed hot keys, collapsing both the tail and the median. On the p99 cannon
(local cut, 2026-06-10) it dominates naive and HELIX across every workload:

| workload | naive p99 | HELIX p99 | **probability p99** | probability p50 |
|----------|----------:|----------:|--------------------:|----------------:|
| burst | 53.6µs | 4.6µs | **4,400ns** | 400ns |
| sustained | 13.9µs | 16.9µs | **1,300ns** | 400ns |
| mixed | 16.8µs | 20.0µs | **4,400ns** | 400ns |

HELIX alone cuts burst p99 from 53.6µs to 4.6µs (a 91.4% regime change);
Probability routing then halves the tail again and collapses p50 to **400
nanoseconds** — an order of magnitude under the naive p50.

Reproduce:

```bash
cargo build --release --manifest-path polyglot/Cargo.toml --bin monster --bin gnexec --bin becky
node gnode/benchmarks/monster-ts-shootout.mjs
node gnode/benchmarks/cross-language-bench.mjs
node gnode/benchmarks/where-does-time-go.mjs --iterations=8 --warmup=3
node gnode/benchmarks/monster-p99-cannon.mjs
```

## Sovereign interpreter shims (`$PATH`)

So agents in this repo cannot quietly burn CPU on a native interpreter, the
monorepo prepends `$MONOREPO_ROOT/bin` to `$PATH` (see
[`open-source/setup.sh`](../../setup.sh) `setup_sovereign_shims` +
`update_path`). That directory shadows `node`, `bun`, `tsx`, `ts-node`, `deno`,
`python`, and `python3` with thin shims that redirect **script execution** onto
the native polyglot binary (`polyglot/target/release/monster`) — FOIL, no
interpreter process. `node foo.ts`, `bun run foo.ts`, and `python foo.py` all run
on the graph runtime; nothing spawns node/bun/python.

This is deliberately the **native** `monster`, not the node-based
`gnosis/bin/monster` launcher (whose `run`/`.ts`/`.js` arms `exec node
gnode.js …` and were the source of stray node/bun CPU). The confined guarded
variant of these shims lives in `open-source/.moonshine.shims` and is used by
moonshine plugin wrappers; the `$PATH` set is the interactive-agent surface.

Policy is FOIL-first with a narrow escape so the documented toolchain stays
alive:

| invocation | routes to |
|---|---|
| `node X.ts` / `bun run X.ts` / `python X.py` / `tsx X.ts` / `deno run X` / `java X.java` / `kotlin X.kt` / `go run X.go` | native `monster` (FOIL) |
| leading flags (`node --version`, `python -c`, `python -m`) | real interpreter |
| `bun build` / `bun x` / `bun test` / package-script `bun run <name>` | real bun |
| `<INTERP>_FORCE_REAL=1` (`NODE_FORCE_REAL`, `BUN_FORCE_REAL`, `PYTHON_FORCE_REAL`) | real interpreter |
| `npx` | real npx passthrough |

`pnpm`, `npm`, `cargo`, and `rustc` are intentionally **not** shimmed: they are
the build/orchestration floor and the repo's documented `pnpm run …` entry
points. The sovereign bootstrap (the `a0` launcher and `monster`'s own
`cargo build`) resolves the real `node`/`cargo` via `resolve_real_node`
regardless of the shims, so the toolchain can always rebuild itself.

When `monster` can't yet express a redirected script (e.g. a bare top-level
`.js`/`.py` with no extractable topology returns `no topologies emitted`), that
is a polyglot coverage gap to land here — not a reason to widen the escape.

## Shims

Monster is a structural runtime — it walks tree-sitter ASTs natively without a JS engine, an FS API, a network stack, or a process model. Anything that touches the host beyond pure computation lives in a *shim*: a stub that recognizes the call shape and returns a placeholder.

**Default policy: fail-closed.** Every shim that doesn't have a real backend raises `GnexecError::Unsupported` (exit code 9) with a message naming the missing capability. This avoids the silent-loss anti-pattern (a sham `200 OK` from a `fetch` that never fired, or a `null` from a `node:fs/promises#appendFile` that dropped the write) — see `feedback_silent_catch_anti_pattern.md` and the 2026-04-13 hft incident write-up for the cost of getting this wrong.

**Permissive opt-in: `GNEXEC_PERMISSIVE_SHIMS=1`.** Set this when you want the legacy null-return behavior — typically only useful for the structural-pass coverage probes the README's bench numbers describe. Production callers should leave it unset.

### Capabilities and current status

| capability | status | what would land it |
|---|---|---|
| `fetch(url, init?)` | fail-closed (was: returned `{ok:true,status:200,body:null}`) | wire `reqwest`/`hyper` into `host_global_function_call`'s `fetch` arm, gate on `--allow-net` |
| `node:fs/promises` (`appendFile`, `readFile`, `writeFile`, `mkdir`, `unlink`) | fail-closed via the module-shim instance dispatch | resolve the import to a real fs backend in `call_instance_method`; gate on `--allow-fs` |
| `node:fs` (sync API) | fail-closed | same as `fs/promises` but on the sync entry points |
| `node:net` (`createConnection`, UDS sockets) | fail-closed | wire `tokio`/`mio` socket; gate on `--allow-net` |
| `node:http` / `node:https` | fail-closed | once `fetch` is real, route through it |
| dynamic `import('...')` of arbitrary module ids | fail-closed | extend `make_opaque_module_shim` resolver to know specific module ids and return real handles |
| Kotlin/JVM `ProcessBuilder` | fail-closed by default; shaped deterministic process only under `GNEXEC_PERMISSIVE_SHIMS=1` | wire a real subprocess backend behind an explicit allow-process capability |
| `process.argv` / `process.env` / `process.cwd()` | implemented (snapshot at startup) | already in `make_process_value` |
| `console.log`/`error`/`warn`/`info`/`debug` | implemented (writes to stderr) | already in `eval_call` member dispatch |
| `Date.now`, `Buffer.from`, `crypto.randomBytes`, `URL`, `JSON.parse`/`stringify`, `Math.*`, `Math.log10` | implemented | already wired |
| `Set` / `Map` / `Uint8Array` / typed arrays | implemented (modeled as plain Object/Array) | already wired |
| Python stdlib module shims (`os`, `argparse`, `json`, `sys`, `re`, `pathlib`, …) | permissive opaque shim — `os.path.join(...)` chains member access without erroring | acceptable for probes; per-module backends would land case-by-case |
| `React` namespace + `useState`/`useRef`/etc hooks | shaped stubs (non-fail-closed because TSX components can't extract without them) | acceptable; component-mount tests want host probes anyway |
| user-defined classes / sibling-file utilities | resolved through the topology registry when the extractor saw the source; otherwise opaque shim | multi-file resolution lives in `multi_file.rs` (opt-in via `GNEXEC_MULTI_FILE=1`) |

### How fail-closed is implemented

Three call sites enforce the policy:

1. `host_global_function_call("fetch", ...)` — the bare-identifier `fetch(url)` site.
2. `call_instance_method` when the receiver is `is_module_shim(...)` — covers `<imported-module>.<method>(...)` patterns like `await appendFile(...)` after `import { appendFile } from 'node:fs/promises'`.
3. `invoke_callable` when the *callee* is a module shim — covers `<imported-module>(...)` callable patterns like `argparse(...)`.
4. `eval_ir_call` when the bare-identifier callee is unbound — covers cases where the import wasn't statically resolvable so the symbol never reached scope.

Each site raises `unsupported_shim(<capability>, <detail>)` which formats a single-line error including the capability name, a hint, and a pointer to this section.
